title: Cohort Analysis SQL Pattern

dialect_traps.construct: DATEDIFF / date subtraction for interval calculation
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: In PostgreSQL: EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (date2 - date1)) / 2592000.0 for months, or DATE_PART('month', AGE(date2, date1)). DATEDIFF does not exist.
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use DATE_TRUNC to snap both dates to month boundaries, then subtract: (DATE_TRUNC('month', activity_date) - DATE_TRUNC('month', signup_date)). Or use EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM ...) / 86400 for days.

dialect_traps.construct: FIRST_VALUE vs MIN for cohort assignment
dialect_traps.postgres_behavior: Both work but MIN(signup_date) in a subquery is cleaner and faster than FIRST_VALUE for cohort assignment
dialect_traps.safe_alternative: Use MIN(date_col) in a subquery to get cohort start date, then join back

mistake_patterns.mistake: Not snapping dates to period boundaries before computing interval
mistake_patterns.symptom: Cohort intervals look wrong — users appear in wrong month buckets
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT user_id, signup_date, activity_date, activity_date - signup_date AS days_active FROM users JOIN activity USING (user_id)
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', signup_date) AS cohort_month, DATE_TRUNC('month', activity_date) AS activity_month, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (DATE_TRUNC('month', activity_date) - DATE_TRUNC('month', signup_date))) / 2592000 AS months_since_signup FROM users JOIN activity USING (user_id)

mistake_patterns.mistake: Counting events instead of distinct users in cohort retention
mistake_patterns.symptom: Retention > 100% because one user generates multiple events
mistake_patterns.bad_sql: SELECT cohort_month, interval_month, COUNT(*) AS retained FROM cohort_activity GROUP BY 1, 2
mistake_patterns.good_sql: SELECT cohort_month, interval_month, COUNT(DISTINCT user_id) AS retained FROM cohort_activity GROUP BY 1, 2

concept_explanation.what_it_does: Cohort analysis groups users by their acquisition date and tracks their behaviour over time. The key steps: (1) assign each user to a cohort month, (2) for each user activity, compute the interval since cohort start, (3) aggregate distinct users per cohort × interval cell.
concept_explanation.mental_model: Think of a grid: rows are cohorts (signup month), columns are intervals (month 0, 1, 2...). Each cell = % of that cohort still active at that interval.

template: WITH cohorts AS (SELECT user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', MIN(created_at)) AS cohort_month FROM users GROUP BY 1), activity AS (SELECT user_id, DATE_TRUNC('month', activity_date) AS active_month FROM user_activity) SELECT c.cohort_month, EXTRACT(EPOCH FROM (a.active_month - c.cohort_month)) / 2592000 AS months_since_start, COUNT(DISTINCT c.user_id) AS retained_users FROM cohorts c JOIN activity a USING (user_id) GROUP BY 1, 2 ORDER BY 1, 2
anti_patterns: Do not use COUNT(*) for retention — always COUNT(DISTINCT user_id)
Do not mix cohort boundaries (e.g. weekly cohort vs monthly activity) without explicit conversion
